does: This paper proposes Auto-Rubric, a training-free framework that converts implicit neural reward models into explicit natural language rubrics. Rather than optimizing continuous neural network weights through gradient descent, the method iteratively learns discriminative evaluation criteria via verification-driven refinement, then compresses these criteria into a compact core set using an information-theoretic coding rate objective. The learned criteria are organized into a hierarchical rubric structure with high-level evaluation dimensions supported by concrete verification checks. Empirically, the approach achieves strong performance with minimal data: using only 70 preference pairs, rubric-guided judges outperform fully trained reward models on diverse benchmarks, with Qwen3-8B reaching 80.91% on RewardBench2.
connects: This work builds on the reward modeling literature that traditionally relies on supervised learning over preference data to train neural reward models, addressing the opacity and data inefficiency of these black-box approaches. It extends recent work on interpretable reward specifications and connects to research on leveraging language models as reward functions through structured prompting. The paper responds to limitations in conventional gradient-based optimization by drawing on principles from discrete optimization, information theory, and natural language generation. It also relates to broader efforts in mechanistic interpretability and alignment to make AI systems' objectives more transparent and auditable, combining ideas from rubric-based evaluation, verification-driven learning, and information-theoretic compression used in summarization and knowledge distillation.
enables: This framework opens directions for developing interpretable, portable, and sample-efficient reward functions that can be shared and debugged more easily than black-box neural models. The explicit rubric representation enables human inspection, modification, and composition of reward criteria across different domains without retraining. The training-free approach points toward reward modeling that scales to new tasks with minimal data, potentially supporting rapid deployment in preference learning pipelines. Future work could explore automatically discovering novel evaluation dimensions, extending rubrics to multi-objective settings, and using these structured specifications for reward hacking detection and robustness certification. The approach also enables broader applications in automated evaluation systems, curriculum learning, and collaborative human-AI alignment where transparent reasoning criteria are essential.
